titleStmt.title: Structural brain network organization in children with prenatal alcohol exposure

sourceDesc.bibl: Xiaoyun Liang, Claire E. Kelly, Chun-Hung Yeh, Thijs Dhollander, Stephen Hearps, Peter J. Anderson, Deanne K. Thompson. (2024). Structural brain network organization in children with prenatal alcohol exposure. NeuroImage: Clinical, 44(None), 103690. DOI: 10.1016/j.nicl.2024.103690

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: 4ae14a7
default
grobid.training.segmentation

text.front:
Clinical Epidemiology and Global Health 25 (2024) 101500
Available online 28 December 2023
2213-3984/© 2023 The Authors.
Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of INDIACLEN. This is an open access article under the CC BY license
(http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
Factors influencing the prevalence of anaemia in female adolescents: A
population-based study of rural setting in Karanganyar, Indonesia
Fathimah Sulistyowati Sigit a,b, * , Fiyan Bahrul Ilmi c, d , Prisilla Desfiandi e , Dewantari Saputri f ,
Nur Dwi Fajarini f , Ana Susianti d , Latifah Agustina Lestari g, h , Asaduddien Faras f
a Department of Public Health Nutrition, Faculty of Public Health, Universitas Indonesia, Depok, Indonesia
b Metabolic, Cardiovascular, and Aging Research Cluster, The Indonesian Medical Education and Research Institute, Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Indonesia, Jakarta,
Indonesia
c West Tegal Community Health Center, Tegal, Indonesia
d Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Islam Sultan Agung, Semarang, Indonesia
e Faculty of Medicine, Public Health, and Nursing, Universitas Gadjah Mada, Yogyakarta, Indonesia
f Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Sebelas Maret, Surakarta, Indonesia
g Eka Hospital, South Tangerang, Indonesia
h Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Trisakti, Jakarta, Indonesia
A R T I C L E I N F O
Keywords:
Anaemia
BMI
Dietary intake
Menstruation
Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC)
Underweight
Women of reproductive age (WRA)
A B S T R A C T
Background: Anaemia among women of reproductive age (15-49 years) poses substantial public health concerns,
particularly in LMICs, due to its adverse effects during pregnancy and childbirth. Understanding its determinants
is pivotal for devising strategies to combat this condition. This study investigates the prevalence of anaemia and
its associations with anthropometric indices, dietary habits, and menstruation status among female adolescents
in Karanganyar, a rural subdistrict in Indonesia.
Methods: In this population-based cross-sectional study, a multi-stage sampling method was employed to
represent all female high-school students in Karanganyar. Participants underwent capillary haemoglobin testing,
anthropometric measurements, and completed dietary intake and menstruation status assessments. Anaemia was
defined as Hb < 12.0 g/dL. Logistic regressions were performed to assess the associations between anthropo-
metric indices, dietary intake, menstruation status, and the prevalence of anaemia.
Results: Approximately 49 % of female adolescents in Karanganyar had anemia. Both BMI and Mid-Upper-Arm-
Circumference exhibited inverse associations with anaemia [AOR(95%CI): 0.87(0.79-0.95) and 0.89
(0.81-0.99)]. Compared to individuals with BMI-for-age-Z-Score (BAZ) > -2 SD, those with BAZ < -2 SD had
more than double the odds of anaemia [2.43 (0.94-6.29)]. Consumption of fruits [0.32(0.10-0.98)] and vege-
tables [0.30(0.11-0.78)] were negatively associated with anaemia. No association was observed between
menstruation status (length, duration, pads/day) and anaemia.
Discussion: High prevalence of anaemia among female adolescents in Karanganyar, and potentially other rural
areas in Indonesia, underscores the need for targeted interventions. Being underweight rapidly increases the risk
of anaemia, emphasizing the importance of nutritional improvements. Consuming iron-rich fruits and vegetables
may prevent anaemia. Menstruation status did not appear to be a determinant of anaemia.
text.body:
1. Introduction
Anaemia is a medical condition characterised by low haemoglobin
levels in the erythrocytes that impairs general health. 1 Haemoglobin
plays a vital role in circulation by binding oxygen in the lungs and
transporting it to tissues throughout the body. When haemoglobin levels
are low, the delivery of oxygen to the brain, muscles, and other vital
tissues is disrupted, leading to fatigue, impaired cognitive functions, and
reduced work performance. 2 These complications in turn are detri-
mental to productivity and the quality of life of the population. 3 Glob-
ally, the World Health Organization estimated in 2019 that
approximately 30 % of women aged 15-49 years were anaemic, 1,4 with
text.front:
* Corresponding author. Department of Public Health Nutrition, Faculty of Public Health, Universitas Indonesia, Depok, Indonesia.
E-mail address: fathimah10@ui.ac.id (F.S. Sigit).
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect
Clinical Epidemiology and Global Health
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/cegh
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cegh.2023.101500
Received 17 September 2023; Received in revised form 7 December 2023; Accepted 21 December 2023
text.body:
75 % of severe cases and anaemia-related mortality occurring in
South-East Asia and Africa regions, which are predominantly low-and
middle-income countries (LMICs). 4,5
Anaemia in women, particularly those of reproductive age, poses a
serious public health threat, as it is associated with detrimental and even
life-threatening complications during pregnancy and childbirth. 2 The
complications include postpartum haemorrhage, premature-born and
low birth weight babies, all contributing to increased risks of infant and
maternal death rate and growth and development retardation. 2,6 Among
women of reproductive age, the global prevalence of anaemia was the
highest in the 15-19 years age group. 4
Although previous studies have shown that diets lacking in essential
micronutrients (e.g., iron, folate, and vitamin B12) are risk factors for
anaemia, 7,8 other factors such as menstruation status in women of
reproductive age remain a subject of debate. 9,10 Globally, it is estimated
that up to a third of women of reproductive age experience heavy
menstrual bleeding, 3 but whether this occurs more frequently and is
associated with anaemia in female adolescents living in rural areas is
still unclear.
Moreover, micronutrient deficiencies are often undiagnosed or
overlooked, as the examination requires specific laboratory blood ex-
aminations that are not always affordable or available in remote areas.
Consequently, more general measures of undernutrition (e.g., BMI, mid-
upper arm circumference) are often the only practical methods to be
used in settings with limited facilities. Furthermore, epidemiological
studies exploring the occurrence and risk factors of anaemia with set-
tings in rural areas of LMICs are still relatively rare. A study has shown
that pregnant women in rural areas have insufficient dietary intakes of
essential nutrients, 11 and thus potentially experience an increased risk of
anaemia.
Hence, this study aims to investigate the prevalence of anaemia and
the associations of dietary intake, menstrual cycle, and anthropometric
indices with anaemia in female adolescents in Karanganyar, a rural
district in Indonesia. By identifying the possible risk factors for anaemia
in Indonesia as one of the most populous LMICs, we hope to contribute
to defining a preventive mass measure for anaemia in women living in
LMICs, especially those in rural settings.
2. Methods
2.1. Study design
This is a cross-sectional study conducted in the subdistrict of Kar-
anganyar, a rural area situated in the Pekalongan Regency, Central Java,
Indonesia. According to the Central Bureau of Statistics, 10.57 % of
households in the area lived below the national poverty line. 12 The study
area encompassed the working vicinity of the Karanganyar Community
Health Center (KCHC), the free-for-public primary healthcare facility
that is fully funded by the government. KCHC routinely has public
health programs driven by the Ministry of Health, that aim to solve
timely health issues in society. This study analyses the secondary data
from the regional surveillance program for anaemia in female adoles-
cents, which was conducted jointly by the local health office and KCHC.
2.2. Data collection
From approximately 1995 female adolescents aged 15-19 who live
in the Karanganyar subdistrict, all female high school students (n =
730), who were generally aged 16-18, were encouraged to participate in
the government-funded anaemia screening. 13,14 Healthcare personnel
from KCHC visited all three high schools in the subdistrict and tested all
female students for their haemoglobin levels using a rapid haemoglo-
binometer. Among the three high schools, the largest school with the
most populous students was visited twice, in which in the second visit all
female students were asked to complete a set of general and lifestyle
questionnaires and underwent a thorough anthropometric
measurement. For this study, we only included female students who
have complete data on the level of haemoglobin, anthropometric mea-
surement (BMI and Mid-Upper Arm Circumference), and have
completed the questionnaires, resulting in a total sample of 240 female
high school students (Supplemental Fig. 1).
2.3. Haemoglobin measurement
A mobile point-of-care test kit for haemoglobin (HemoCue®
Hemoglobinometer) was used to measure the level of haemoglobin in all
participants using a capillary blood sample. Anaemia was defined ac-
cording to the WHO criteria as haemoglobin level below 12.0 g/dL.
We further categorised the severity of anaemia according to the WHO
classification for non-pregnant women aged ≥15 years, which are Hb
11.0-11.9 g/dL for mild anaemia, 8.0-10.9 g/dL for moderate anaemia,
and <8.0 g/dL for severe anaemia. 15
2.4. Anthropometric measurements
In all respondents, body mass index (BMI) and mid-upper arm
circumference (MUAC) were measured. BMI is defined as weight in ki-
logram, divided by the square of height in meters. Weight was measured
by a digital scale to the nearest 0.1 kg, whereas height was measured
with a portable stadiometer to the nearest 0.1 cm. MUAC was measured
with a flexible tape measure to the nearest 0.1 cm, and was measured
halfway between shoulder and elbow. MUAC is used as a proxy of un-
dernutrition status, with MUAC<23 cm is defined as having a chronic
energy deficiency. 16,17
2.5. The general and lifestyle questionnaires
Participants were asked to complete a set of questionnaires to pro-
vide data on general sociodemographic information, dietary intake,
physical activity habits, and menstrual cycles. Dietary intake was
assessed using a simplified food frequency questionnaire, which
measured the frequency and quantity of consumption of common food
products. For physical activity, information on the frequency and
duration of exercise was recorded. Adequate physical activity was
defined according to the national healthy lifestyle guideline as engaging
in moderate to intense physical activity for at least 30 min per day or
150 min per week. 18,19
While the accurate assessment of blood loss during menstrual
bleeding is challenging, with no consensus available for quantifying it,
to gain insight into the quantity of blood loss during the menstrual cycle,
the number of sanitary pads used per day during menstruation were
asked. In addition, the length, regularity, and the first occurrence
(menarche) of the menstrual cycle were also asked.
2.6. Statistical analyses
Descriptive characteristics of the population were presented as mean
(SD) or median (25th-75th percentiles) for continuous variables and
percentage (95 % CI) for categorical variables. All analyses were per-
formed using STATA™ statistical software program version 17.
Multivariable logistic regression analyses were conducted to
examine the associations of anthropometric indices (BMI, MUAC),
menstruation status, and dietary intake with anaemia. We adjusted these
associations for sociodemographic and lifestyle confounding factors,
including age, parents' occupation, household size/number of family
members, physical activity, and (passive) smoking. The association be-
tween menstruation status and anaemia was additionally adjusted for
BMI, whereas the association between dietary intake and anaemia was
further mutually adjusted by each food category. The estimates were
presented as odds ratios with 95 % confidence intervals.
text.note:
F.S. Sigit et al.
Clinical Epidemiology and Global Health 25 (2024) 101500
text.body:
2.7. Ethics and approval
This program is a derivation from the national anaemia prevention
and iron supplementation program by the Indonesian Ministry of Health
(PERMENKES No. 88/2014, and PERMENKES No. 51/2016). 20 One
week prior to the anaemia surveillance, an announcement letter from
KCHC was provided to all female students regarding the surveillance
plan. Although encouraged to participate, the letter also stated that
students could opt out of the study if they or their parents did not wish to
participate. In addition, one day before the survey, an oral reminder was
given via announcement from the homeroom teachers in each class-
room, and students were instructed to relay the information and discuss
their participation with their parents.
On the morning of surveillance day before data collection began, all
female adolescents were given an information session by healthcare
professionals to educate them about anaemia and its health and societal
consequences. They were given ample time and opportunities to ask
questions regarding anaemia and the study they were about to enrol in.
All participants agreed and signed informed consent. KCHC had
reviewed and given permission for their data to be used in this study
(Approval No. 156 441/893/2023).
3. Results
3.1. Descriptive characteristics of the study population
The mean (SD) age of participants was 16.2 (0.9) years. Nearly half
(49 %) of the study population had a haemoglobin level of less than 12
g/dL, of which 2 % had severe, 21 % moderate, and 26 % mild anaemia.
Almost ten percent (9.2 %) of the population had a BMI-for-age Z-score
(BAZ) of < -2 SD.
In regard to parental occupation, 7 % of participants reported that
their parents worked as government or private employees, 9 % as factory
workers, 14 % as farmers, 42 % as entrepreneurs, and 27 % as others.
Only half of the participants had three regular meals daily, and the soy-
based Tofu/Tempeh appeared to be regularly consumed by the majority
of participants. The median duration of physical activity per week was
30.0 (21.3-45.0) minutes, and 74 % of participants had at least one
member in their household smoking at home.
All participants have already menstruating, but almost a third (27 %)
have irregular cycle. A third (34 %) have cycle that are less than a
month. On average, the participants changed sanitary pads three times
[2.9 (0.8)] daily during their menstrual cycle (Table 1).
3.2. The associations of anthropometric indices, dietary intake, and
menstruation status with anaemia
BMI was inversely associated with anaemia, and the association
remained stable after adjustment for sociodemographic and lifestyle
confounding factors [adjusted Odds Ratio (95 % CI): 0.87 (0.79-0.95)].
Similarly, an inverse association was also found in MUAC, with an
adjusted OR (95 % CI) for anaemia of 0.89 (0.81-0.99). When catego-
rising BMI into underweight (BAZ < -2 SD) or not underweight (BAZ >
-2 SD), those with underweight had more than double the odds of
having anaemia compared to those without, despite borderline signifi-
cant [adjusted OR (95 % CI): 2.43 (0.94-6.29)](Table 2).
While the consumption of poultry and meats was inversely associ-
ated with anaemia, all crude and adjusted odds ratios had confidence
intervals that were crossing 1 [adjusted OR (95 % CI): 0.69 (0.23-2.03)].
Consuming fruit and vegetable were negatively associated with anaemia
[adjusted ORs (95 % CIs): 0.32 (0.10-0.98) and 0.30 (0.11-0.78)], and
these association remained stable even after additional adjustment for
BMI and other food categories. We did not observe a clear association
between menstruation status with anaemia, as all crude and adjusted
odds ratios had confidence intervals that crossed 1 (Table 2).
4. Discussion
In this study, we investigated the prevalence of anaemia, as well as
the associations of anthropometric indices, dietary intake, and
menstruation status with anaemia in female adolescent in a rural setting
in Indonesia. We observed that almost half (49 %) of the population had
anaemia. BMI and MUAC, as well as consuming fruits and vegetables,
were inversely associated with anaemia. We did not find evidence that
menstruation (menarche, bleeding quantity, duration, and regularity of
cycle) was associated with anaemia.
The high prevalence of anaemia that was observed in this study was
in line with the global and national estimated prevalence. The World
Bank estimated that more than a third of women of reproductive age,
particularly those aged 15-19 and living in LMICs, suffer from (iron
deficiency) anaemia. 5 Compared to the national prevalence, we
observed that the prevalence of anaemia in our study was higher than
that of the Indonesian National Health Survey, which was roughly 32
%. 21 This is probably due to the national survey averaging the level of
anaemia in urban and rural areas, whereas in our study, the participants
lived entirely in rural settings.
Of all predictors, the strongest association was seen between BMI and
anaemia. In particular, we observed that compared to those with normal
weight, those who were underweight had more than double the risk of
anaemia. We also observed that MUAC, another measure of malnour-
ishment, was inversely associated with anaemia. The inverse associa-
tions between anthropometric indices (BMI and MUAC) and anaemia
may be explained by the common micronutrient deficiencies in in-
dividuals with underweight (BAZ < -2 SD) and chronic energy
Table 1

2 (1-5)

20.0 ± 3.2

23.7 ± 2.7
Physical Activity, minutes/week (median, 25th-75th percentile) b

Smoker, yes (%, 95 % CI)
0
Passive Smoker, yes (%, 95 % CI)
74 (68-79)
Menstruation Status

99 (97-100)
Menarche, years (mean ± SD)
12.7 ± 1.0
Regular cycle, yes (%, 95 % CI)
72 (66-78)
Length of cycle, days (mean ± SD)
3.0 ± 1.3
>1 cycle/month, yes (%, 95 % CI)
35 (29-41)
Sanitary pads per day, pads (mean ± SD)
2.9 ± 0.8
Dietary Intake
Having 3 regular meals per day (%, 95 % CI)
54 (47-60)
2 regular meals per day (%, 95 % CI)
42 (36-49)
Trying to lose weight (%, 95 % CI)
10 (6-14)

11 (7-15)

10 (7-15)
Eggs, >3 portions a week (%, 95 % CI)
27 (22-33)
Soybean (Tofu/Tempeh), >3 portions a week (%, 95 % CI)
47 (41-54)
Fruits, >3 portions a week (%, 95 % CI)
14 (10-19)
Vegetables, >3 portions a week (%, 95 % CI)
35 (29-42)
Coffee/Tea, >3 portions a week (%, 95 % CI)
18 (14-24)
Milk, or other dairy products, >3 portions a week (%, 95 % CI)
18 (13-23)
Data are presented in mean (SD) or median (25th-75th percentiles) for contin-
uous variable, and in proportion (%) for categorical variable.
a Anemia is defined according to WHO criteria of Hb < 12 g/dL.
b Physically active as defined by the national healthy lifestyle guideline
'GERMAS': moderate to intense physical activity of more than 30 min/day or
150 min/week.
text.note:
F.S. Sigit et al.
Clinical Epidemiology and Global Health 25 (2024) 101500
text.body:
deficiency (MUAC<23 cm). In acute or chronic malnourishment, the
individuals commonly experience not only macronutrients but also
micronutrients deficiency. 7,8 Deficiencies in essential micronutrients
such as iron, vitamin B12, or folate were among the common causes of
anaemia, as these micronutrients play a vital role in the haematopoiesis,
particularly the regeneration of new erythrocytes. 22,23
In regard to dietary intake, we observed that consuming fruits and
vegetables was protective against anaemia. Although in the present
study the types of fruits and vegetables were not recorded, previous
studies have shown that green leafy vegetables are a rich source of iron,
which supports the regeneration of haemoglobin24,25. Vitamin-C-rich
fruits and vegetables are also known to help the absorption of iron in
the gastrointestinal tract. 24,25 Nevertheless, despite being known to be
the primary source of iron, we did not find strong evidence that

Data are presented as OR (95 % CI). Model 1 is adjusted for sociodemographic confounding factors (age, household size/number of family member, and parental
occupation). Model 2 is adjusted for Model 1, plus additional adjustment for lifestyle variables (duration of physical activity and (passive) smoking. Model 3 is adjusted
for Model 2, plus additional adjustment for BMI. Model 4 is adjusted for Model 3, plus additional adjustment for all other food categories.
text.note:
F.S. Sigit et al.
Clinical Epidemiology and Global Health 25 (2024) 101500
text.body:
consuming animal-based food products (e.g., beef, poultry, and fish)
more than three portions per week was protective against anaemia, as
the majority of confidence intervals of the associations were crossing 1,
although the effect sizes were <1.
Then in regard to menstruation, we did not find evidence that
menstruation (menarche, bleeding quantity, and duration and regularity
of cycle) was associated with anaemia. These were partially in line with
previous literature, as studies investigating the associations between
menstruation and anaemia often observed conflicting results. For
example, our findings were consistent with an earlier study that
observed no association between menstruation status and anaemia. 26
However, several other studies have shown that heavy menstrual
bleeding was associated with iron loss and, in severe cases, iron defi-
ciency anaemia. 10,27 Other studies have also observed that menstrual
length and frequency were associated with increased risks of anaemia
and decreased level of ferritin3,9,28. Thus, it is still recommended that
intervention to reduce menstrual blood loss should not be overlooked,
particularly in those experiencing heavy menstrual bleeding, 10 as up to a
third of women of reproductive age experience heavy menstrual
bleeding. 3
4.1. Strength and limitations
The strength of the study is its ability to provide an overview of
anaemia and its determinants among female adolescents in rural
Indonesia, in which studies showing findings from this setting is quite
rare. However, there are several limitations of this study. First, the cross-
sectional study design implies that no causality can be concluded from
the associations. Additionally, due to the observational nature of the
present study, there is a possibility of residual confounding that may
distort the true exposure-outcome associations. Second, there was po-
tential social desirability bias as participants may provide socially ex-
pected answers for the self-reported questionnaires (e.g., lifestyle).
Third, as the researchers visited the high schools to collect data, the
diagnosis of anaemia was measured only with a portable haemoglo-
binometer. Hence, a microscopic blood smear or complete blood count
test could not be performed, and we could not identify the specific
causes of anaemia in each individual. Fourth, the use of the Food Fre-
quency Questionnaire (FFQ) to assess dietary intake in this study may
lead to unreliable measures of total diet, total energy intake, and
nutrient values. However, as consumption of animal-based food prod-
ucts was very low in the study population and the Indonesian population
in general, we believe that FFQ is still the most relevant instrument as
other types of practical dietary assessment (e.g., 24-h food recall) may
lead to overestimation of intake if animal-based protein sources were
mentioned in the record.
4.2. Research implications
Despite the limitations, this study lays the groundwork for further
research. To help establish the definitive causes of anaemia in each in-
dividual, we encourage further studies to incorporate more thorough
laboratory examinations, such as microscopic blood smear test to
examine the morphology of erythrocytes and the mean corpuscular
volume. Screening for anaemia-inducing infectious diseases that are
common in LMICs, such as helminth and malarial infection may be
important, particularly because these infections are associated with
blood loss and iron deficiency in the infected individuals.
The high prevalence of anaemia in this study population provides
insight into the gravity of anaemia as a public health issue in Indonesia,
particularly among female adolescents living in rural areas. As results
from this study mainly represent the area of Karanganyar and its sur-
roundings, further studies with more rural areas could better show the
severity of the problem at the national level.
4.3. Public health implications
There are several public health implications of this study. First, as we
observed a prevalence of anaemia of 49 % in the female adolescent
population, this reflects a severe public health significance based on the
WHO classification. 15 As also shown by previous studies that anaemia in
women of reproductive age is the most common in adolescents, 4 public
health policy planning should be prioritised for this target group. Sec-
ond, as we observed that anaemia in the present study was mainly
explained by measures of undernutrition (BMI and MUAC), an
improvement in nutrition status could lead to anaemia reduction.
Improving nutrition status in women of reproductive age would also
eventually benefit in preventing chronic energy deficiency, which is
unfortunately a common nutrition-related problem in pregnant women
in Indonesia. 29 As previous research indicates that iron deficiency is the
leading cause in LMICs, accounting for up to 50 % of cases globally, 4,30
efforts such as iron supplementation or fortification in staple foods with
a blanket approach should be encouraged.
5. Conclusions
In conclusion, this study highlights the alarmingly high prevalence of
anaemia among female adolescents in Karanganyar, and potentially
other rural areas in Indonesia. BMI and MUAC were strongly inversely
associated with anaemia, and being underweight doubled the risk of
anaemia. Consuming iron-rich fruits and vegetables may offer benefits
to prevent anaemia. We did not find evidence that menstruation was
associated with anaemia. Implementing multisectoral collaboration to
eliminate anaemia is crucial, and female adolescents living in rural areas
should be prioritised for targeted anaemia intervention.
text.div:
Ethical Approval statement
The ethics of this study have been reviewed and approved by The
Research and Community Engagement Ethical Committee, Faculty of
Public Health, Universitas Indonesia (No.: Ket-811/UN2.F10.D11/
PPM.00.02/2023).

Funding
This study receives no funding.

Author contribution statement
FSS -Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal Analysis, Investiga-
tion, Resources, Data Curation, Writing -Original Draft, Visualization,
Project Administration.
FBI -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.
PD -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.
DS -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.
NDF -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.
AS -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.
LAL -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.
AF -Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation,
Writing -Review & Editing, Project Administration.

Declaration of competing interest
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial
interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence
text.note:
F.S. Sigit et al.
Clinical Epidemiology and Global Health 25 (2024) 101500
text.div: the work reported in this paper.

Acknowledgement
The authors thank Dr. Suryadi, Mr. Bowo Prasetijo, SST, and all
Karanganyar Community Health Center staff for their enormous support
and valuable partnership in sharing the surveillance data that makes this
publication possible.

Appendix A. Supplementary data
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.
org/10.1016/j.cegh.2023.101500.

t/data/gho/data/themes/topics/anaemia_in_women_and_children.

women of reproductive age in low-and middle-income countries between 2000 and
2018. Nat Med. 2021 Oct 1;27(10):1761-1782.

the Management of iron deficiency and iron-deficiency anemia in women with

women ages 15-49) [Internet] https://genderdata.worldbank.org/indicators/sh

reproductive-age females in the Tharu tribe of the Indo-Nepal border region. J Fam
